does: This paper addresses the safety tax—the performance degradation in reasoning ability that often accompanies safety alignment in language models. The authors propose On-Policy Self-Distillation for safety Alignment (OPSA), which trains models on their own generated rollouts rather than fixed external demonstrations. The method uses a frozen teacher copy conditioned on privileged safety context to provide dense per-token KL supervision, directing the student model toward safer reasoning. A key innovation is the introduction of teacher flip rate, a metric quantifying how often a privileged context transforms unsafe responses into safe ones. Rather than simply eliciting surface-level safe outputs, this signal identifies contexts that activate latent safety reasoning. Experiments across two reasoning-model families and five different scales demonstrate that OPSA reduces the safety-reasoning tradeoff.
connects: This work builds on extensive research into safety alignment of large language models, extending beyond standard supervised fine-tuning approaches that suffer from distributional mismatch between training data and model deployments. It engages with the literature on self-distillation and on-policy learning in reinforcement learning, applying these principles to safety training. The paper connects to work on constitutional AI and preference-based alignment that use external supervision signals, while advancing toward more principled on-policy methods. It also relates to research examining interpretability and latent capabilities in language models, particularly the idea that safety knowledge exists but may not be properly activated. The safety tax problem itself has been documented across various alignment approaches, making this a direct response to an observed limitation in the field.
enables: This work enables more efficient safety alignment that preserves model reasoning capabilities, potentially allowing practitioners to scale safety techniques to larger models without the traditional performance cost. The teacher flip rate provides a novel quantitative signal for searching over safety contexts, which could inspire additional research into discovering optimal prompting strategies or context designs for safety. The on-policy self-distillation framework suggests future directions in combining safety alignment with other learning paradigms like reinforcement learning from human feedback. The approach points toward better understanding how models can be trained to access and utilize latent safety reasoning, potentially informing interpretability research. More broadly, success here could influence how industry approaches safety-capability tradeoffs, making it more feasible to deploy genuinely capable yet safe systems at scale.
